narrative.canopy: At 10 metres above sea level, this is the lowest of all five origins and the flattest. No altitude to shape the growing conditions. Warm and humid nearly year-round — cultivation works differently here, not against cold and mist, but with heat and wet.
narrative.land: Ginger here is grown at homestead scale — small household plots, ginger grown alongside whatever else a family is already raising, not as a single dedicated crop. This mixed, small-plot character is partly why the ginger tastes the way it does: grown without the uniformity a plantation tries to impose.
narrative.hand: Unlike most of what we source, ginger isn't picked — it's dug up. The whole rhizome is lifted from the soil, then boiled briefly, then sun-dried over several days until most of the water is gone. The drying is the defining act: fresh ginger and dry ginger are not the same thing one dried out. They are different materials.
